one_line_takeaway: R2-Guard adds an explicit weighted first-order-logic reasoning layer (via MLN or an efficient clustered probabilistic circuit) on top of category-specific unsafety detectors, beating eight guardrail baselines on six datasets (0.869 vs 0.816 avg AUPRC) and dramatically improving jailbreak robustness (0.987 vs 0.747 UDR on AdvBench GCG/AutoDAN).
key_finding: Explicit logical reasoning over category correlations improves both clean detection and jailbreak robustness over purely data-driven guardrails, and the efficient PC variant matches MLN accuracy at a fraction of the cost. R2-Guard (MLN and PC) reaches 0.869 average AUPRC across six datasets vs 0.816 for the best baseline (ensemble), and attains near-perfect UDR against GCG/AutoDAN attacks (avg 0.987 MLN / 0.986 PC on AdvBench) where the best baseline reaches 0.747. Pseudo-learning performs on par with real-learning, and learned rule weights correlate with empirical category correlations (Pearson 0.801).
core_contribution: R2-Guard, an LLM input/output guardrail that decouples detection into two parts: (1) a data-driven category-specific learning component that outputs a per-category unsafety probability vector, and (2) a knowledge-enhanced reasoning component that encodes relationships among safety categories as first-order logical rules (direct rules like 'self-harm => unsafe' and indirect rules like 'self-harm/instructions => self-harm') with learnable weights, compiled into a probabilistic graphical model that performs explicit probabilistic inference to compute P['unsafe'=1 | x]. The paper instantiates the reasoning component with two PGM types, Markov Logic Networks (MLNs) and Probabilistic Circuits (PCs), and contributes a spectral-clustering-based layered PC structure for efficiency, two knowledge-weight learning schemes (pseudo-learning on simulated data and real-learning on labeled data), and a new pairwise-constructed stress-test benchmark, TwinSafety.
threat_model: The guardrail is deployed to moderate LLM input/output; an adversary crafts jailbreak prompts that try to bypass detection while still conveying unsafe content. The paper evaluates against a white-box adaptive attack (GCG, optimizing adversarial suffixes via token gradients, including universal suffixes GCG-U1/U2, suffixes optimized on Vicuna-7B (GCG-V), on LlamaGuard (GCG-L), and on a distilled Gemma-2B copy of R2-Guard (GCG-R)) and black-box attacks (AutoDAN genetic search, and LLM-driven PAIR and TAP). It also targets the non-adversarial failure of existing guardrails: poor handling of correlated/long-tail safety categories and inflexibility to new categories.
limitations: The paper's own Discussion notes R2-Guard requires explicit human specification of safety-knowledge rules (annotating detailed categories and their interconnections), unlike purely data-driven models. Robustness degrades sharply against reformulation-style black-box attacks: in Table 4, average UDR drops on PAIR (0.581/0.583) and TAP (0.375/0.369), which the authors attribute to those attacks making prompts semantically less harmful, and they call for fairer benchmarks. R2-Guard also inherits the quality of the underlying category-specific detectors (it reuses OpenAI Mod / LlamaGuard / ToxicChat-T5), and MLN inference is exponential in the number of categories, motivating the PC approximation.

datasets_used: TwinSafety (new pairwise-constructed benchmark introduced in this paper; 7 categories: intent-hiding, mismatched-context, proverb-usage, double-entendre, media-usage, science-purpose, virtual-context)
AdvBench (unsafe-only set used for jailbreak UDR evaluation)
quantitative_results: Average AUPRC across six datasets: R2-Guard MLN 0.869 and PC 0.869, vs Ensemble 0.816, ToxicChat-T5 0.776, LlamaGuard 0.772, OpenAI Mod 0.733, CoT 0.712, Azure 0.693, Perspective 0.641, Detoxify 0.587 (Table 2)
ToxicChat AUPRC: R2-Guard PC 0.909 / MLN 0.903 vs LlamaGuard 0.698 (~30.2% relative gain, matching abstract claim)
OpenAI Mod AUPRC: R2-Guard MLN 0.926 / PC 0.924 (Table 2)
TwinSafety AUPRC: R2-Guard MLN 0.758 / PC 0.757, best baseline LlamaGuard 0.737 (lowest scores overall, showing benchmark difficulty)

AdvBench UDR average over GCG/AutoDAN attacks: R2-Guard MLN 0.987 / PC 0.986 vs Ensemble 0.747, LlamaGuard 0.619, OpenAI Mod 0.442, ToxicChat-T5 0.405; abstract's 59.5% gain = 0.987/0.619 relative over LlamaGuard (Table 3)
R2-Guard achieves UDR 1.000 against GCG-U1, GCG-U2, GCG-V, and GCG-L; drops only to 0.973 under adaptive GCG-R and ~0.945-0.948 under AutoDAN
Under black-box reformulation attacks (Table 4), R2-Guard UDR falls to PAIR 0.581/0.583 and TAP 0.375/0.369, still above all baselines; overall avg 0.860 (MLN) / 0.859 (PC)
Efficiency (Table 5): PC reasoning matches MLN accuracy (both 0.869 avg AUPRC) using 0.0062 s vs 0.1123 s reasoning time per instance (~6% of MLN cost)
Learned rule weights correlate with empirical category-correlations at Pearson coefficient 0.801 (real-learning on BeaverTails)
Per-instance total runtime ~1.45 s (MLN) / 1.35 s (PC), comparable to LlamaGuard's 1.34 s (Table 2)
baselines_compared: Ensemble learning guardrail (max over category-specific scores)
